name: Angelica
description: A powerful guardian herb burned or carried for protection against malevolent forces. Sprinkle the root around the home to ward off negativity.
safety: Avoid during pregnancy. Photosensitizing.
nativity: Northern Europe, Greenland, Central Asia
medical_use: Carminative and digestive antispasmodic. Used in European herbal medicine for indigestion, gas, and loss of appetite.
historic_use: Used in Scandinavian folk medicine since the Viking era. Medieval Europeans chewed the root to ward off plague. Charlemagne grew it in his imperial gardens.

name: Basil
description: A staple of prosperity and love magic, basil is placed in cash registers or wallets to attract money and carried to bring harmony in relationships.
nativity: South Asia, Southeast Asia (native to India)
medical_use: Anti-inflammatory and antibacterial. Used in Ayurveda for respiratory infections, stress, and digestive complaints. Eugenol content gives mild analgesic properties.
historic_use: Sacred to Vishnu in Hinduism; the holy basil (tulsi) variety is worshipped. In ancient Greece it symbolised mourning; in Italy, love. Used in Roman and medieval European medicine.

name: Bay Laurel
description: The laurel of ancient oracles. Write wishes on bay leaves and burn them to send desires to the divine. Used widely for prophetic dreams.
nativity: Mediterranean region
medical_use: Carminative, mild analgesic, and antibacterial. Used in Mediterranean folk medicine for joint pain, digestive disorders, and as a topical anti-inflammatory.
historic_use: Sacred to Apollo. The Pythia at Delphi burned bay leaves for prophetic visions. Ancient Greek and Roman victors were crowned with laurel wreaths — hence 'poet laureate' and 'baccalaureate'.

name: Calendula
description: Strewn beneath the bed to bring prophetic dreams or placed in garlands for protection. Known to strengthen psychic sight when placed around candles.
nativity: Southern Europe, Western Asia
medical_use: Anti-inflammatory, antiseptic, and vulnerary. Widely used in wound-healing creams and lotions. Clinically studied for dermatitis, wound repair, and oral mucositis from cancer treatment.
historic_use: Cultivated in medieval monastery gardens across Europe. Roman cooks added petals to stews and salads. Egyptians prized it as a rejuvenating herb. Dedicated to the Virgin Mary in Christian tradition.

name: Catnip
description: Used in love sachets and to attract a familiar spirit. Catnip is also grown near the home to draw good luck and foster joyful energy.
nativity: Europe, Central Asia
medical_use: Mild sedative and carminative. Used in European and American folk medicine for childhood fevers, colic, and anxiety. Mild diaphoretic (promotes sweating).
historic_use: Cultivated in medieval monastery herb gardens. American frontier settlers used it as a children's fever and colic remedy. Nepetalactone — the compound cats respond to — also repels insects.

name: Cedar
description: A sacred tree of strength and purification burned to cleanse spaces and offer prayers. Cedar chips are placed in chests to protect valuables.
nativity: Lebanon, Western Himalayas; Red cedar native to North America
medical_use: Antimicrobial and expectorant. Eastern red cedar was used by Indigenous North Americans for respiratory illness. Cedar oil used topically as an antiseptic and insect repellent.
historic_use: Cedar of Lebanon was used to build Solomon's Temple and the palaces of ancient Mesopotamia. Sacred to Enlil in Sumeria. Indigenous peoples of the Pacific Northwest consider it a relative, not a resource.

name: Chamomile
description: Wash hands in chamomile tea before gambling or games of chance to attract luck. A cup before bed invites restful sleep and pleasant dreams.
nativity: Europe, Western Asia; widely naturalised worldwide
medical_use: Anti-inflammatory, antispasmodic, and mild sedative. One of the most studied medicinal herbs. Used clinically for anxiety, insomnia, IBS, and infantile colic. Apigenin binds to GABA receptors.
historic_use: Listed in the Ebers Papyrus (1550 BCE) for treating fever. Sacred to Ra in Egypt. Included in the Anglo-Saxon Nine Herbs Charm. One of the most widely used medicinal plants in medieval European medicine.

name: Cinnamon
description: One of the most potent magical spices for raising the vibration of a working. Burned as incense to attract money and accelerate any spell.
safety: Cinnamon bark oil is a strong irritant. Use with care on skin.
nativity: Sri Lanka (Ceylon), South India
medical_use: Studied for blood sugar regulation in type 2 diabetes. Antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory. Cinnamaldehyde has demonstrated activity against bacterial and fungal infections.
historic_use: One of the earliest traded spices in recorded history. Used in ancient Egyptian embalming. Mentioned in the Song of Solomon. The quest for cinnamon and other spices directly drove European maritime exploration in the 15th–16th centuries.

name: Clove
description: Burned to stop others from speaking ill of you and carried as a charm for friendship. Whole cloves are added to sachets to drive out hostile forces.
nativity: Maluku Islands (Spice Islands), Indonesia
medical_use: Analgesic and antimicrobial. Eugenol is an active component used in dental anaesthesia and toothache remedies. Antifungal and antibacterial properties well documented.
historic_use: Archaeological finds date clove use to 1721 BCE in Syria. The Spice Islands were the sole source for centuries, driving fierce European colonial competition. Indonesian kretek cigarettes blend cloves with tobacco.

name: Dandelion
description: Blowing dandelion seeds carries wishes and messages to the spirit world. A tea of the root drunk before divination is said to enhance the gift of sight.
nativity: Eurasia; now cosmopolitan worldwide
medical_use: Mild diuretic and liver tonic. Rich in vitamins A, C, and K. Roasted root used as a caffeine-free coffee substitute. Studied for anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties.
historic_use: Used medicinally in Chinese medicine for over 1,000 years. Arab physicians in the 10th century CE described it. European settlers introduced it intentionally to the Americas as a food and medicine crop.

name: Dragon's Blood
description: A resin from the Dracaena palm that amplifies the power of any working it is added to. Burned alone for protection or combined with other resins to increase their potency.
safety: Highly flammable. Use caution on charcoal.
nativity: Canary Islands (Dracaena draco); Southeast Asia and Sumatra (Daemonorops draco)
medical_use: Used in traditional medicine as an astringent for wound healing and stopping bleeding. Latin American sangre de drago (Croton lechleri) is used similarly for gastrointestinal complaints and wound care.
historic_use: Prized in ancient Rome and medieval Europe as a pigment, varnish, and medicine. Used in ancient Canarian culture by the Guanche people. Arab traders supplied it to European apothecaries from the 1st century CE onward.

name: Elder
description: The Elder Mother is a spirit who must be asked permission before harvesting. Used in rites of transition, healing, and warding off evil.
safety: Raw berries, bark, leaves and roots are toxic. Use only ripe cooked berries or dried flowers.
nativity: Europe, Western Asia, North Africa
medical_use: Elderberry syrup and extracts are widely used for immune support. Antiviral properties demonstrated in studies. Elderflower is a traditional diaphoretic used for colds and flu.
historic_use: Hippocrates called it his 'medicine chest'. The Elder Mother — Hyldemor in Danish, Frau Holle in German tradition — was a spirit requiring ritual permission before harvesting. Sacred in Slavic, Norse, and British folk tradition.

name: Frankincense
description: A resin burned since antiquity to sanctify sacred space, drive away negativity, and lift consciousness toward the divine. Excellent for consecrating tools.
nativity: Oman, Yemen, Somalia, Ethiopia
medical_use: Boswellic acids are anti-inflammatory and studied for arthritis, Crohn's disease, and asthma. Used in Ayurveda as shallaki for joint conditions. Neuroprotective properties under investigation.
historic_use: Traded for over 5,000 years; one of the most valuable commodities of the ancient world. Used continuously in Egyptian and Babylonian temples. Mentioned over 50 times in the Bible. One of the three gifts of the Magi.

name: Ginger
description: A fiery root that speeds up and sharpens any spell it is added to. Carried to attract adventure and adventure in love, or used in money workings.
nativity: Southeast Asia; likely originating in the Indian subcontinent
medical_use: Anti-nausea, anti-inflammatory, and carminative. Clinically demonstrated efficacy for morning sickness and chemotherapy-induced nausea. Gingerols and shogaols inhibit prostaglandin synthesis, providing anti-inflammatory effects.
historic_use: One of the earliest traded spices; mentioned in Chinese texts over 2,500 years ago. Used in Ayurvedic medicine as a universal medicine (vishwabhesaj). Arab traders controlled the ginger trade for centuries before European maritime routes were opened.

name: Hawthorn
description: A threshold tree — a gateway between worlds and a home for the fae. Berries are used in love charms and branches hung over doors for protection.
safety: Seeds contain cyanogenic compounds; do not eat. Has significant heart-medicine interactions.
nativity: Europe, northwestern Africa, western Asia
medical_use: Well-studied cardiovascular herb. Hawthorn berry extracts are used in European phytomedicine for mild heart failure and angina. Oligomeric proanthocyanidins improve coronary circulation and have antioxidant effects.
historic_use: The May blossom was central to Beltane celebrations across Celtic Europe. In Irish tradition, lone hawthorns growing on hilltops are fairy trees and must never be felled. Used in ancient Greek wedding ceremonies as a symbol of hope.

name: Hyssop
description: One of the most ancient purifying herbs. Sprinkle hyssop-infused water through a space or over a person to cleanse guilt, grief, and spiritual contamination.
safety: Avoid in pregnancy and epilepsy. Essential oil is toxic.
nativity: Southern Europe, Middle East, Central Asia
medical_use: Expectorant and antispasmodic. Used in European herbal medicine for bronchitis, coughs, and respiratory catarrh. Contains pinocamphone and isopinocamphone, which have demonstrated antimicrobial activity.
historic_use: Mentioned in the Old Testament (Psalm 51) for ritual purification: 'Purge me with hyssop, and I shall be clean.' Used in ancient Hebrew rites to sprinkle the blood of the Passover lamb. Included in medieval European herbal pharmacopoeias.

name: Juniper
description: Burned to drive out negative entities and disease. Juniper berries are strung and hung as protective charms or added to sachets for love.
safety: Avoid therapeutic use in pregnancy and kidney disease. Berries are safe culinary use.
nativity: Circumpolar; widespread across Europe, Asia, and North America
medical_use: Diuretic, antiseptic, and carminative. Berries used in European herbal medicine for urinary tract infections and digestive complaints. Active component terpinen-4-ol has demonstrated antibacterial properties.
historic_use: Burned in Tibetan and Native American purification rites. Ancient Egyptians burned it in temple fumigation. The primary flavoring agent in gin — the English word derives from French genièvre (juniper). Used as a fumigant during plague in medieval Europe.

name: Lavender
description: Among the most versatile magical herbs in any tradition. Used to invite peaceful sleep, soothe anxiety, attract love, and purify the aura.
nativity: Mediterranean region, Middle East, India
medical_use: Anxiolytic and mild sedative. Lavender oil (Silexan) is clinically proven effective for generalised anxiety disorder. Used for insomnia, headaches, and minor burns and skin irritation.
historic_use: The name derives from Latin lavare — to wash. Romans perfumed bathwater and bed linen with it. Used in medieval European hospitals to combat infection. Strewn on floors as a disinfectant and insect repellent.

name: Lemon Balm
description: A gentle herb of the Moon that uplifts the spirit and soothes grief. Used in healing magic and spells for new love, and to enhance lunar workings.
nativity: Mediterranean region, Central Asia
medical_use: Mild anxiolytic, antispasmodic, and antiviral. Clinically studied for anxiety, insomnia, and agitation in dementia. Rosmarinic acid inhibits COMT, extending the calming effect of catecholamines. Antiviral against herpes simplex.
historic_use: The genus name Melissa is Greek for honeybee — bees were said to cluster on it. Avicenna praised it in the Canon of Medicine for lifting the spirits and strengthening the heart. Carmelite water, a 14th-century remedy for nerves and headaches, was distilled from lemon balm.

name: Mint
description: Placed in the wallet to draw money or rubbed on cash before spending. Mint tea is consumed to open psychic channels and freshen spiritual energy.
nativity: Europe, Middle East, Central Asia
medical_use: Antispasmodic, carminative, and analgesic. Peppermint oil used clinically for IBS, tension headaches (topical), and nausea. Menthol is a widely used active ingredient in analgesic products.
historic_use: Found dried in ancient Egyptian tombs. Romans used it to flavour wine and cleanse banquet tables. In Greek myth, the nymph Minthe was transformed into the plant by Persephone. Pliny listed 41 medicinal uses.

name: Mugwort
description: The witch's herb par excellence — placed under the pillow for vivid dreams, burned before scrying, and carried to strengthen all psychic work.
safety: Avoid during pregnancy. May cause allergic reactions in those sensitive to ragweed.
nativity: Eurasia, North Africa; naturalised in North America
medical_use: Central to moxibustion in Traditional Chinese Medicine, where burning moxa cones on acupoints treats pain and cold conditions. Mild uterine stimulant used to regulate menstruation in European herbal medicine.
historic_use: One of the Nine Sacred Herbs of Anglo-Saxon tradition. Roman soldiers placed it in their sandals to prevent fatigue on marches. Used in the ancient Chinese texts over 2,000 years old. Central to the Japanese Obon festival.

name: Myrrh
description: A sacred resin burned for consecration, healing, and contacting the ancestors. Often combined with frankincense to create a deeply purifying incense.
safety: Avoid in pregnancy. Do not use medicinally with blood thinners.
nativity: Eastern Africa (Somalia, Ethiopia), Arabian Peninsula
medical_use: Antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory, and vulnerary. Used in Egyptian and Ayurvedic medicine for wound healing, oral infections, and as a gargle for sore throats. Sesquiterpenes have demonstrated analgesic and anti-inflammatory properties.
historic_use: One of the most valuable commodities of the ancient world — used continuously in Egyptian embalming and temple rites for over 4,000 years. One of the gifts of the Magi. Used in Jewish temple incense (ketoret) and mentioned throughout the Song of Solomon. Arab traders monopolized the supply for centuries.

name: Nettle
description: A fierce protective herb — scattered about a space to drive out unwanted spirits or stuffed in a poppet to turn back a hex.
safety: Wear gloves when handling fresh plant. Cook or dry thoroughly before consuming.
nativity: Europe, Asia; now cosmopolitan worldwide
medical_use: Anti-inflammatory and nutritive. High in iron, calcium, and vitamins A and C. Clinically studied for benign prostatic hyperplasia and allergic rhinitis. Root extracts used for prostate health in European phytomedicine.
historic_use: One of the most ancient European food and fiber plants — nettle cloth predates linen in northern Europe. Roman soldiers urticated (flogged) their limbs with nettles in cold climates to restore circulation. Listed as a food and medicine in medieval herbals across Europe.

name: Patchouli
description: A deeply earthy scent that anchors workings in the physical plane. Used in money drawing, fertility magic, and to ground spiritual energy into tangible results.
nativity: Tropical Asia; native to the Philippines and Malaysia
medical_use: Antiseptic, antifungal, and anti-inflammatory. Used in traditional Chinese and Ayurvedic medicine for skin conditions including eczema, acne, and dermatitis. The essential oil is studied for its antimicrobial properties.
historic_use: Used in Southeast Asia to scent textiles, particularly silk and shawls exported to Europe. Victorian-era consumers associated the smell with genuine Indian Kashmir shawls; counterfeit goods lacked the scent. Became iconic in 1960s counterculture as a signature scent of the hippie movement.

name: Rose
description: The supreme flower of Venus — petals are scattered in love baths, rosehips carried for luck, and rosewater used to consecrate tools and altars.
nativity: Asia; cultivated worldwide for over 5,000 years
medical_use: Rosehip tea is exceptionally high in vitamin C. Rosewater used as mild anti-inflammatory for skin and eyes. Rose oil in aromatherapy studied for reducing depression and anxiety.
historic_use: Sacred to Aphrodite and Venus; to the Virgin Mary in Christianity. Sub rosa ('under the rose') meant secrecy. Cleopatra reportedly filled a reception room knee-deep in rose petals. Cultivated since at least 3000 BCE.

name: Rosemary
description: Where rosemary grows, the woman rules the house — it is placed under pillows for memory, burned for purification, and used in healing sachets.
nativity: Mediterranean region
medical_use: Antioxidant and anti-inflammatory. Rosmarinic acid has demonstrated antimicrobial properties. Studies suggest rosemary aroma improves working memory and alertness. Used traditionally for headaches and hair loss.
historic_use: Used in ancient Greek and Roman funeral rites as a memory herb. Shakespeare's Ophelia says 'there's rosemary, that's for remembrance.' Burned in sick rooms during medieval plague outbreaks as a fumigant.

name: Rue
description: A sharp and fierce protective herb — worn as a charm against the evil eye or added to purification baths to remove psychic attacks and curses.
safety: Photosensitizing — avoid sun after skin contact. Toxic in large doses. Avoid in pregnancy.
nativity: Balkan Peninsula, southwestern Asia; widely naturalised in Mediterranean region
medical_use: Antispasmodic and emmenagogue. Used in traditional European medicine for eye complaints, rheumatism, and as an abortifacient. Contains rutin, a bioflavonoid used to strengthen capillaries. Not recommended for internal use due to toxicity.
historic_use: One of the most important magical and medicinal herbs of classical antiquity — praised by Pliny and Dioscorides. Shakespeare's Ophelia and the gardener in Richard II both reference it as the 'herb of grace.' In Italian folk tradition the cimaruta (sprig of rue) charm is the pre-eminent amulet against the evil eye (malocchio).

name: Sage
description: Burned to cleanse a space of stagnant or negative energy before ritual work. Also used in wisdom spells and to promote clear thinking.
safety: White sage is an over-harvested plant. Consider growing your own or using ethically sourced alternatives.
nativity: Mediterranean region
medical_use: Antimicrobial, astringent, and antihidrotic. Clinically studied for reducing menopausal hot flashes and excessive sweating. Used for sore throats and oral infections. Mild cognitive enhancer studied for Alzheimer's prevention.
historic_use: The name Salvia derives from Latin salvare — to save or heal. Romans considered it a sacred herb. Medieval saying: 'Cur moriatur homo cui Salvia crescit in horto?' (Why should a man die who grows sage in his garden?)

name: Sandalwood
description: A sacred wood burned to aid meditation, raise vibrations, and carry wishes to the spirit world. One of the most universal sacred scents.
safety: True sandalwood is endangered — use sustainably sourced material.
nativity: South India (Karnataka, Tamil Nadu); also East Timor and Pacific islands
medical_use: Antiseptic, anti-inflammatory, and mild sedative. Sandalwood oil used in Ayurveda for urinary tract infections and skin inflammation. Alpha-santalol demonstrates antimicrobial properties and is studied for skin conditions including acne.
historic_use: Used continuously in Hindu and Buddhist temple ritual for over 4,000 years — the paste applied to deities and burned before shrines. One of the principal ingredients in Indian Ayurvedic preparations. The trade in Indian sandalwood was controlled by the Mysore royal family for centuries.

name: St. John's Wort
description: Hung above doors and windows at midsummer to ward away evil spirits and harmful magic. A solar herb for courage and clarity.
safety: Significant drug interactions — can reduce effectiveness of many medications including contraceptives. Photosensitizing.
nativity: Europe, western Asia, North Africa; naturalised worldwide
medical_use: Antidepressant. Multiple meta-analyses show efficacy comparable to standard antidepressants for mild to moderate depression. Hypericin and hyperforin are the primary active compounds. Induces CYP450 enzymes — significant interactions with many drugs.
historic_use: Harvested on St. John's Day (midsummer, June 24) in European folk tradition and hung above doors to ward off evil. Used in ancient Greek medicine by Hippocrates and Dioscorides. The red oil extracted from the flowers was called the 'blood of St. John' and used for wounds.

name: Sweetgrass
description: Braided and burned to invite benevolent spirits and ancestors. Unlike sage it attracts positive energy rather than driving out negative energy.
nativity: Northern North America, northern Eurasia (circumpolar distribution)
medical_use: Used in Indigenous North American medicine as a smoke treatment for coughs and colds, and as a topical wash for skin conditions. Coumarin compounds give the grass its characteristic sweet scent.
historic_use: One of the most sacred plants in many Plains and Woodland Indigenous traditions, representing the hair of Mother Earth. Central to smudging ceremonies across numerous nations. Braided sweetgrass is offered to elders and used in basket weaving traditions. In European tradition (Poland), used to flavor Żubrówka vodka.

name: Thyme
description: Worn by warriors for courage and burned by healers for purification — thyme placed under the pillow brings restful sleep and visions of a future lover.
nativity: Southern Europe, Mediterranean region
medical_use: Antispasmodic and expectorant. Thymol is an active component of many modern antiseptic mouthwashes and household disinfectants. Clinically used for bronchitis and upper respiratory infections in European phytomedicine.
historic_use: Ancient Egyptians used it in embalming. Greeks burned it in temple offerings. Roman soldiers bathed in thyme water for vigour before battle. Scottish Highlanders drank thyme tea for courage and to prevent nightmares.

name: Valerian
description: Called 'all-heal' by the ancients — used in love sachets and placed in the home to prevent discord. A powerful herb for deep sleep and peace.
safety: May cause drowsiness. Avoid with sedative medications.
nativity: Europe, parts of Asia
medical_use: Sedative and anxiolytic. Widely used for insomnia and anxiety in European herbal medicine. Multiple clinical trials have assessed its efficacy for sleep latency reduction, with mixed but generally positive results.
historic_use: Hippocrates described its properties. Used in ancient Rome as a perfume. During WWI and WWII it was widely used to treat shell shock and anxiety in soldiers. The strong smell attracts cats similarly to catnip.

name: Vervain
description: A sacred herb of the druids and the Romans — gathered at midsummer and used to consecrate altars, anoint tools, and bless the space of working.
safety: Avoid in pregnancy.
nativity: Europe, North Africa, western Asia
medical_use: Nervine, mild antispasmodic, and diaphoretic. Used in European herbal medicine for nervous exhaustion, depression, and anxiety. Mild bitter tonic; used for liver and digestive complaints in traditional medicine.
historic_use: Called herba sacra (sacred herb) by the Romans, used to purify temples and altars. Druids gathered it with the same reverence as mistletoe — at night, when the dog star rose, with iron tools forbidden. Listed as one of the three most sacred Druidic plants alongside mistletoe and water mint.

name: Wormwood
description: Burned to summon spirits and enhance scrying. Wormwood is placed in sachets to protect travelers and carried to call upon the spirits of the dead.
safety: Toxic in large amounts — use only in small quantities for incense. Avoid in pregnancy. Contains thujone.
nativity: Eurasia, North Africa; naturalised in North America
medical_use: Bitter tonic, antiparasitic, and digestive stimulant. Historically used to expel intestinal worms (hence the name). Thujone is a GABA antagonist and convulsant in high doses. Used in very small amounts in digestive bitters formulas.
historic_use: The primary flavoring herb of absinthe — the notorious 'green fairy' drink of 19th-century Parisian bohemian culture, favored by Verlaine, Baudelaire, Van Gogh, and Wilde. Associated with visions and madness; banned in France 1915–2011. Used as a worm expellant since ancient Egypt.

name: Yarrow
description: The ancient wound-herb of Achilles — held in the hand to banish fear, used in divination (classic I Ching), and carried in the wedding bouquet for love.
safety: May cause allergic reactions in those sensitive to aster family plants.
nativity: Temperate regions of the Northern Hemisphere; cosmopolitan
medical_use: Hemostatic, anti-inflammatory, and antispasmodic. Used in Western and Chinese herbal medicine for wounds, fevers, and heavy menstruation. Topical astringent for nosebleeds and minor cuts.
historic_use: Named for Achilles, who used it to stanch his soldiers' wounds. Yarrow pollen was found in a 60,000-year-old Neanderthal burial at Shanidar Cave in Iraq — among the earliest evidence of intentional plant use by humans. Classic stalks used for I Ching divination.

name: Belladonna
description: Named for Atropos — she of the three Fates who cuts the thread of life. One of the central herbs of the European witchcraft tradition, used in flying ointments to induce visionary trance states. Renaissance women used diluted preparations to dilate the pupils; bella donna means 'beautiful woman.'
safety: Safe for spell jars, sachets, and altar work. Do not ingest — all parts are toxic. Avoid prolonged skin contact with bruised plant matter or sap; gloves recommended when handling fresh material. Do not burn indoors. Keep away from children and animals.
nativity: Central and southern Europe, western Asia, North Africa
medical_use: Atropine (derived from belladonna) is an essential medicine on the WHO Model List. Used in medicine to dilate pupils for eye examination, treat bradycardia, reverse organophosphate poisoning, and as a pre-operative antispasmodic. Scopolamine (also from belladonna) is used for motion sickness and nausea patches.
historic_use: Renaissance Italian women used diluted belladonna eye drops to dilate their pupils for cosmetic effect — hence 'bella donna' (beautiful woman). One of the witches' classic 'flying ointment' plants alongside henbane, datura, and mandrake. Associated with Hecate in Greek tradition. Macbeth's soldiers supposedly used it to drug the Danish army's wine during a truce.

name: Hemlock
description: The poison of Socrates — used in Greek executions and deeply embedded in the darker currents of European folk magic. Associated with binding, endings, and the authority of Saturn over all living things. Not to be confused with the hemlock tree, which is unrelated.
safety: Safe for spell jars and sealed workings. Do not ingest under any circumstances — causes progressive paralysis and respiratory failure. Wash hands after handling; avoid touching eyes or mouth. Easily confused with wild carrot and parsley — identification must be certain before harvesting wild.
nativity: Europe, North Africa, western Asia; widely naturalised in North America
medical_use: No current medicinal use — too toxic for therapeutic application. Historically used in tiny doses as a sedative and for spastic conditions. Coniine (the principal alkaloid) was the first alkaloid to be synthesized in a laboratory. Strictly a historical reference only.
historic_use: The state poison of ancient Athens — Socrates was executed with hemlock in 399 BCE. Plato's Phaedo describes the progressive numbness rising from the feet upward. Used by the Celts in poison-tipped arrows. Associated in European folk magic with Hecate and the darker traditions. Medieval herbalists described it cautiously; an Italian Renaissance poisoner's herb.

name: Henbane
description: A classic herb of the witch's garden — used alongside belladonna and datura in the legendary flying ointments of European witchcraft. The smoke was inhaled for oracular trance. Associated with Hecate, the underworld, and the crossing of boundaries between worlds.
safety: Safe for spell jars, sachets, and altar work. Do not ingest — causes delirium and potentially fatal respiratory depression. Wear gloves when handling significant quantities of fresh plant material. Historically many deaths from accidental consumption; keep well away from children and animals.
nativity: Europe, western Asia, North Africa; naturalised widely
medical_use: Hyoscyamine and scopolamine from henbane have legitimate medical applications — used as antispasmodics, pre-operative sedatives, and in motion sickness patches. These are now derived from cultivated sources or synthesized. The raw plant is too unpredictably toxic for safe therapeutic use.
historic_use: Seeds found at the Iron Age site of Danebury hillfort suggest ritual use in Britain over 2,000 years ago. Henbane seeds mixed with beer were the likely cause of mass intoxication events in medieval Europe. Used by the Pythia at Delphi (alongside bay laurel) as a potential oracular plant according to some classical sources. Hamlet's father was 'poisoned with hebenon' — scholars widely identify this as henbane.

name: Datura
description: Called toloache in Mexican tradition and used in brujería, curanderismo, and the vision rites of numerous Indigenous peoples of the Americas. Among the most dangerous psychoactive plants on Earth — the line between a visionary dose and a lethal one is extremely thin. Associated with the dark moon, shapeshifting, and deep Otherworld travel.
safety: Safe for spell jars and sealed workings; do not burn. Do not ingest under any circumstances — alkaloid levels vary wildly between individual plants and deaths are common even among experienced users. Wash hands thoroughly after handling. This entry is for spiritual and historical reference only.
nativity: Datura stramonium likely from Central America or Asia (disputed); D. inoxia from Mexico and southwestern North America
medical_use: Scopolamine and atropine derived from Datura species have legitimate medical applications — now obtained from cultivated or synthetic sources. Datura was historically used in Ayurveda as an antispasmodic and anesthetic. The raw plant is far too variable and dangerous for any therapeutic use.
historic_use: D. inoxia (toloache) was central to initiation ceremonies of the Chumash and other California peoples, as well as Mexican curanderismo and brujería. Carlos Castaneda controversially described its use (as 'devil's weed') in his Yaqui sorcery books. D. stramonium arrived in Jamestown, Virginia — colonists accidentally ingested it and the resulting mass delirium gave 'Jimsonweed' its name ('Jamestown weed').

name: Mandrake
description: Perhaps the single most celebrated magical plant in the Western tradition. Its forked root resembles a human figure — it was harvested at midnight with elaborate ritual, said to scream on uprooting. Used as a familiar spirit, a poppet base, and in some of the most powerful love and protection workings in European folk magic.
safety: Safe for poppet work, spell jars, and altar use. Do not ingest — all parts contain tropane alkaloids. The berries are appealing but toxic. Avoid prolonged bare-skin contact with fresh root. Note: American mayapple (Podophyllum) is often sold as 'mandrake' and is a different plant entirely.
nativity: Mediterranean region, Middle East
medical_use: Historically used as an anesthetic — combined with wine for pre-surgical sedation in ancient and medieval medicine. Contains scopolamine, atropine, and hyoscyamine. Dioscorides described mandragoras wine for pain during surgery. No current clinical use due to extreme toxicity risk.
historic_use: No plant has a richer magical history. Described by Theophrastus, Josephus, and Pliny with elaborate harvesting rituals. The Talmud and medieval Jewish sources describe the dudaim (mandrakes) Rachel bargained for from Leah to increase fertility (Genesis 30). Shakespeare's Romeo and Juliet, The Winter's Tale, and Othello all reference it. Prized in medieval Europe as a familiar spirit and love charm — the 'little man of the gallows,' said to grow beneath the feet of hanged men.

name: Wolfsbane
description: One of the most potent poisons in the European flora — used by hunters to tip arrows and deeply embedded in the lore of werewolves and shapeshifters. Associated with Hecate, who was said to have created it from the saliva of Cerberus. Used in protective and binding workings against those who work in animal forms.
safety: Safe for spell jars and sealed workings. Always wear gloves — aconitine absorbs through skin and is one of the most potent natural toxins known. Wash immediately after any contact. Do not burn or ingest. No antidote exists for poisoning. Widely considered the most dangerous plant in the British Isles.
nativity: Mountains of central and western Europe, from the Pyrenees through the Alps to the Carpathians
medical_use: Aconitine is used in homeopathic preparations (Aconite) for acute fear, fever onset, and shock — at infinitesimal homeopathic dilutions only. Chinese aconite (Aconitum carmichaelii — fuzi) is used in Traditional Chinese Medicine after detoxification processing. The raw plant has no safe therapeutic use — aconitine is lethal in doses as small as 1–2 mg.
historic_use: Called 'the queen of poisons' in antiquity. In Greek mythology, created by Hecate from the foam of Cerberus's mouth. Used by hunters across Eurasia to poison arrow and spear tips (hence 'wolfsbane'). Medea attempted to poison Theseus with it. Associated with werewolf transformation in European folklore — both the trigger and the cure.

name: Foxglove
description: The 'folk's glove' — fairy folk's glove. One of the most powerful fairy plants in British and Celtic tradition, associated with the Otherworld and the hidden folk. Worn or carried to attract fairy contact and to see through glamours. The spotted interior of the flowers is said to be fairy fingerprints.
safety: Safe for spell work and altar use. Do not ingest — cardiac glycosides cause fatal arrhythmia. Wash hands after handling. The leaves closely resemble comfrey; do not confuse when wildcrafting. Toxic to animals — keep clear of pets.
nativity: Western and central Europe, northwestern Africa
medical_use: Digitoxin and digoxin (cardiac glycosides) derived from foxglove leaves are essential medicines for heart failure and atrial fibrillation. William Withering's 1785 Account of the Foxglove established cardiac medicine as a science. Digoxin remains on the WHO Model List of Essential Medicines. The therapeutic window is extremely narrow — the effective dose is close to the toxic dose.
historic_use: William Withering discovered its cardiac use in 1775 after an elderly Shropshire woman cured his patient's dropsy (heart failure edema) with a secret herbal recipe — he identified foxglove as the active ingredient. Before Withering, it was used in Welsh folk medicine as a topical remedy for skin sores. The fairy lore — spots are fairy fingerprints, bending flowers marks the path of fairies — is widespread in British Isles tradition.

name: Peyote
description: A small spineless cactus sacred to dozens of Indigenous peoples of Mexico and the American Southwest for at least 5,000 years. The sacrament of the Native American Church. The Huichol (Wixáritari) make annual pilgrimages hundreds of miles to harvest it in the sacred land of Wirikuta. It is a living deity to those traditions, not merely a plant.
safety: Schedule I in the US except for enrolled members of federally recognized tribes using it in bona fide religious ceremony. Critically endangered due to overharvesting. Approaching this plant with anything less than profound respect for its living traditions is a serious ethical failure. This entry is for historical and spiritual reference only.
nativity: Chihuahuan Desert; southern Texas and northern Mexico
medical_use: Mescaline (the primary alkaloid) is under current clinical investigation for treatment-resistant depression, PTSD, and addiction, following the broader psychedelic research renaissance. Historical research from the 1950s–60s showed promise for psychiatric treatment. All current use remains in research protocols due to Schedule I status.
historic_use: Peyote use by Indigenous peoples of northern Mexico dates to at least 5,700 years based on archeological evidence from the Shumla Caves in Texas. The Native American Church, founded around 1890, incorporates peyote as its central sacrament and now has over 250,000 members across North America. The Wixáritari (Huichol) regard the annual pilgrimage to Wirikuta to harvest hikuri as among the most sacred acts a person can undertake.

name: Agrimony
description: A protective herb carried to guard against poisons and ill spirits. Placed under the pillow to bring nightmare-free sleep; used in witch bottles to return hexes to their sender.
nativity: Europe, western Asia, North Africa
medical_use: Astringent, mild diuretic, and cholagogue. Used in European herbal medicine for diarrhea, sore throats (as a gargle), and liver conditions. The tannin content gives it astringent wound-healing properties.
historic_use: Recorded in the Anglo-Saxon herbal Leechbook of Bald (10th century CE) as a remedy for wounds. Used in medieval England for 'ague' (malaria) and as a wound herb by surgeons. Dioscorides recommended it for liver, kidney, and snake bites.

name: Alder
description: A threshold tree of the water's edge — between worlds. In Celtic tradition the alder is sacred to Bran, used in rites of transition, truth-telling, and weather working.
nativity: Europe, southwestern Asia, North Africa
medical_use: Astringent and febrifuge. Bark tinctures used in traditional European medicine for sore throats (gargle), skin inflammations, and as a topical astringent for wounds. Nitrogen-fixing roots improve soil fertility.
historic_use: Sacred to the god Bran in Celtic mythology — his cauldron of resurrection is associated with the alder. In Irish Brehon law, felling an alder was one of the most serious offenses. Alder wood does not rot underwater; used for the pilings of Venice and other ancient structures built over water.

name: Ash
description: Yggdrasil — the World Tree of Norse tradition — is an ash. Burned in Yule logs, carved into runic staves, and used to bless newborns across Northern European tradition.
nativity: Europe, southwestern Asia
medical_use: Bark used as a febrifuge and mild laxative in European folk medicine. Leaves used as a diuretic and anti-inflammatory. Historically used as a quinine substitute for treating malaria.
historic_use: Yggdrasil, the World Tree connecting the nine realms of Norse cosmology, is an ash. Odin hung for nine days upon it to gain the runes. In Irish mythology, three of the five sacred trees of Ireland were ashes. Welsh and Scandinavian newborns were given ash sap as their first drink.

name: Benzoin
description: A resin burned in Southeast Asian and Western traditions for purification and to uplift consciousness. Combined with frankincense it creates a powerful clarifying incense for meditation and astral work.
nativity: Sumatra, Thailand, Java (tropical Southeast Asia)
medical_use: Antiseptic and expectorant. Compound tincture of benzoin (Friars' Balsam) is used as an inhalant for bronchitis and as a topical protectant for skin irritation. Benzoic acid is a preservative and antimicrobial agent.
historic_use: Traded from Southeast Asia to the Arab world and Europe from at least the 15th century CE. Named luban jawi (incense of Java) in Arabic — 'jawi' was corrupted to 'benjui' in Portuguese and then 'benzoin' in English. Used in church incense across Southeast Asia and incorporated into Western perfumery and medicine.

name: Birch
description: The Lady of the Woods — the first tree to leaf in spring. Birch bundles swept spaces clean before ritual; birch bark was used for spirit writing and Imbolc working.
nativity: Europe, northern Asia; one of the most widespread trees in the Northern Hemisphere
medical_use: Diuretic and anti-inflammatory. Birch leaf tea used in European herbal medicine for urinary tract infections, gout, and rheumatism. Birch sap tapped in spring as a nutritive tonic. Betulin from bark bark has demonstrated anti-inflammatory and antiviral properties.
historic_use: The first letter of the Ogham alphabet is Beith (birch). In Celtic tradition birch is the tree of beginnings, used to bless the new year and newborns. In Siberian shamanism the birch is the World Tree and shamans ascend its notches to reach the upper worlds. Birch bark was used as paper across northern Eurasia — ancient Sanskrit and Russian manuscripts survive on it.

name: Black Cohosh
description: A North American woodland plant used by Indigenous nations in ceremony. Magically associated with drawing courage and breaking hexes; the dark root is carried as a protection charm.
safety: Avoid in pregnancy and with liver conditions. Not for ingestion.
nativity: Eastern North America (Appalachian Mountains, eastern woodlands)
medical_use: Used for menopausal symptoms including hot flashes and night sweats. Multiple clinical trials support modest efficacy. Mechanism may involve serotonergic rather than oestrogenic pathways. Rare hepatotoxicity cases reported — use with caution.
historic_use: Used by Algonquin, Cherokee, and Iroquois nations for menstrual and menopausal complaints, rheumatism, and as a ceremonial herb. Introduced to European settlers who adopted it widely. Became a major ingredient in 19th-century American patent medicines for 'female complaints.'

name: Blackthorn
description: The dark twin of the hawthorn — a tree of the waning year and the harsh face of fate. The thorns were used in binding, cursing, and protective workings in Celtic tradition; the sloes ripen only after the first frost.
nativity: Europe, western Asia, northwestern Africa
medical_use: Astringent and mild laxative. Sloe berries used in European folk medicine for sore throats (gargle) and as a tonic. Flower tea used as a mild diuretic and laxative. The berries flavor sloe gin.
historic_use: Used across Celtic and Germanic traditions as the tree of fate and hardship — in contrast to the hawthorn's association with Beltane and spring, the blackthorn rules the dark half of the year. The Irish shillelagh fighting stick was traditionally made from blackthorn. In Scottish folklore the Cailleach (winter hag) carries a blackthorn staff.

name: Borage
description: Its old name is herb of gladness — floated in ritual drinks and carried to inspire courage before difficult endeavors. A Jupiterian herb that expands the spirit and clears the mind for true seeing.
nativity: Mediterranean region; possibly native to Syria
medical_use: Adrenal tonic and anti-inflammatory. Borage seed oil is exceptionally high in gamma-linolenic acid (GLA) — used for eczema, rheumatoid arthritis, and PMS. Leaves contain pyrrolizidine alkaloids; seed oil does not. Used as an adrenal restorative for stress and burnout.
historic_use: The Roman naturalist Pliny wrote that borage in wine 'maketh men merry and joyful.' Gerard's Herbal (1597) states 'those of our time use the flowers in salads to exhilarate and make the mind glad.' The Welsh word for borage, llawenlys, means 'herb of gladness.' Floated in the 'cups' (traditional punch) at medieval feasts.

name: Burdock
description: The clinging bur root carries symbolic cleansing power — dragged around a property to gather and remove negativity. Added to uncrossing baths and protective sachets.
nativity: Eurasia; naturalised in North America and Australia
medical_use: Alterative, diuretic, and mild hepatoprotective. Root (gobo) is a significant food vegetable in Japan and Korea. Used in Western and Chinese herbal medicine as a blood purifier for skin conditions including psoriasis and eczema. Inulin content supports beneficial gut bacteria.
historic_use: Used in European and Chinese medicine for at least 2,000 years. Root was a staple food in medieval Europe during famine. The hooked burs famously inspired George de Mestral to invent Velcro in 1941. Used in traditional Iroquois and Ojibwe medicine for skin and rheumatic conditions.

name: Camphor
description: A powerfully cooling and clarifying scent burned to clear the mind before divination and purify ritual space. Camphor placed in a room creates a strong barrier against negative entities.
safety: Do not ingest camphor oil — toxic. Use in ventilated spaces only.
nativity: East Asia (China, Japan, Taiwan); cultivated throughout tropical Asia
medical_use: Topical analgesic, counter-irritant, and mild antiseptic. Used in mentholated rubs for muscle pain and respiratory congestion. Camphor is an FDA-approved topical analgesic. Internal use is toxic — even small doses can cause seizures.
historic_use: One of the most important aromatics traded on the Silk Road. Arab physician Avicenna used it for fevers and plague. Marco Polo described Bornean camphor as more valuable than gold. Used in Chinese medicine for over 1,000 years. The scent is used in Hindu and Buddhist temple ritual across South and Southeast Asia.

name: Cardamom
description: An aromatic spice burned and carried for love magic across the Middle East and South Asia — used to warm the heart and draw romantic attraction into sachets and offerings.
nativity: South India (Western Ghats); cultivated in Guatemala, Tanzania, and Sri Lanka
medical_use: Carminative, digestive stimulant, and mild antispasmodic. Used in Ayurveda for indigestion, nausea, and bad breath. Studies suggest cardamom extract may lower blood pressure. Used to counter the addictive quality of chewing tobacco in South Asian traditions.
historic_use: One of the oldest known spices — used in ancient Egyptian ritual and perfumery. Greeks and Romans imported it extensively from India. Arab traders carried it throughout the Islamic world; it remains a defining flavor of Arabic coffee (qahwa) and sweet rice dishes. Known as the 'Queen of Spices' in India.

name: Copal
description: The sacred resin of Mesoamerica — burned continuously in Aztec and Maya temples as an offering to the sun and burned at Día de los Muertos altars to guide ancestors home.
nativity: Mexico, Central America
medical_use: Antiseptic and anti-inflammatory. Used in traditional Mesoamerican medicine for skin infections, joint pain, and respiratory complaints. The smoke has been used for fumigation and wound sterilization in indigenous medicine.
historic_use: The Nahuatl word copalli simply means 'incense.' Burned in vast quantities in Aztec temples — the Chronicles of the Conquest describe smoke rising continuously from Tenochtitlan's Great Temple. Used in Maya ceremony for over 3,000 years. Remains central to Día de los Muertos altars and curanderismo practice throughout Mexico and Guatemala.

name: Cypress
description: The mourning tree of the Mediterranean — planted in cemeteries from Greece to Persia as a marker of the threshold between worlds. Burned to ease grief and guide the passage of the dead.
nativity: Eastern Mediterranean, from Turkey through Iran
medical_use: Astringent and venous tonic. Cypress essential oil and bark extracts used in European phytomedicine for varicose veins, haemorrhoids, and poor venous circulation. The essential oil is used in aromatherapy for respiratory conditions.
historic_use: Sacred to Hades and Pluto — the mourning tree of antiquity. The Roman myth of Cyparissus explains its origin as a boy transformed into a cypress by Apollo after accidentally killing his beloved deer. Planted in cemeteries from ancient Greece through Islamic Persia. The sempervirens (ever-living) species name reflects its ancient association with immortality.

name: Damiana
description: A Mexican herb burned and smoked in love and lust workings for centuries. Mildly entheogenic — small amounts induce mild euphoria and heighten psychic sensitivity for divination.
safety: Legal in most countries. May interact with diabetes medications. Use in moderation.
nativity: Mexico, Central America, Caribbean, South America
medical_use: Mild aphrodisiac, anxiolytic, and antidepressant. Used in traditional Mexican and Caribbean medicine for sexual dysfunction, anxiety, and depression. Apigenin and arbutin are among the active compounds. Studies show modest adaptogenic effects.
historic_use: Used as an aphrodisiac and tonic by the indigenous peoples of Mexico and the Yucatan Peninsula long before European contact. Early Spanish missionaries documented its use in love potions. The original margarita is said to have been made with damiana liqueur in Baja California; a damiana liqueur is still sold in Mexico in a bottle shaped like a pre-Columbian fertility goddess.

name: Dill
description: Hung above doors in the Middle Ages to guard against witchcraft and ill-wishing. Seeds carried in sachets bring luck; fresh dill placed in the bath is said to make one irresistible.
nativity: Mediterranean region, southwestern Asia
medical_use: Carminative, antispasmodic, and mild sedative. Dill water (gripe water) has been used for centuries to relieve infant colic. Carvone and limonene are the principal active compounds. Used in Ayurveda for digestive disorders and lactation support.
historic_use: Found dried in ancient Egyptian tombs. Mentioned in the Ebers Papyrus (1550 BCE). Romans ate it at gladiatorial contests and burned it in celebration. 'Meeting seeds' — dill and fennel seeds chewed in church services during long sermons to suppress hunger and quiet growling stomachs, a practice recorded in Puritan New England.

name: Eucalyptus
description: A powerful Australian cleansing plant — branches hung in showers create healing steam; burned to clear confusion and bring healing energies into a space.
safety: Eucalyptus oil is toxic if ingested. Keep away from cats and small animals.
nativity: Australia and Tasmania
medical_use: Expectorant, decongestant, and antimicrobial. Cineole (eucalyptol) is the primary active compound and is an approved expectorant in many countries. Used in inhalants, throat lozenges, and chest rubs. Antifungal and antibacterial properties well documented.
historic_use: Used medicinally by Aboriginal Australians for thousands of years — leaves applied to wounds, steam inhaled for fever and congestion. Introduced to Europe in the 19th century; planted extensively in Mediterranean countries to drain swamps and combat malaria (hence 'fever tree'). One of the fastest-growing trees on earth.

name: Fern
description: Fern seed — the invisible seed of a plant with no visible seeds — was said in European folk magic to confer invisibility on midsummer's eve. Grown near the home to draw luck.
nativity: Widespread across temperate Northern Hemisphere; bracken fern is nearly cosmopolitan
medical_use: Male fern (Dryopteris filix-mas) root was historically used as a taenicide (tapeworm expellant) — a significant but toxic treatment requiring careful dosing. Bracken fronds (young fiddleheads) are eaten in Japan and Korea but contain carcinogenic compounds; regular consumption is not recommended.
historic_use: Because ferns reproduce via invisible spores (not visible seeds or flowers), European folklore held that collecting 'fern seed' on midsummer's eve granted invisibility — Shakespeare references this in Henry IV. The fronds of bracken were used to thatch buildings and as bedding for livestock across medieval Europe.

name: Fig
description: Sacred to Dionysus and to the Buddha (enlightened beneath a fig tree). Figs represent abundance, wisdom, and the sweetness of divine revelation across Mediterranean and Asian traditions.
nativity: Western Asia (Turkey, Syria); cultivated throughout the Mediterranean for at least 11,000 years
medical_use: Nutritive, mild laxative, and demulcent. High in fiber, potassium, and calcium. Dried figs used in traditional medicine for constipation, respiratory complaints, and skin sores (as a poultice). Ficin enzymes have digestive and anti-inflammatory properties.
historic_use: The common fig is one of the first plants cultivated by humans — domesticated remains found at Gilgal I in the Jordan Valley date to 9400 BCE, predating grain cultivation. Sacred to Dionysus (the fig was the first plant he brought from the underworld). The Bodhi tree under which the Buddha attained enlightenment is a sacred fig (Ficus religiosa). The founding of Rome is tied to the Ficus Ruminalis, the sacred fig under which Romulus and Remus were suckled.

name: Galangal
description: A fiery Asian root used in Hoodoo court case magic — carried in a red flannel bag when facing legal troubles. Also called Low John the Conqueror; burned for protection and good fortune.
nativity: Southeast Asia (Indonesia, Thailand, Malaysia); cultivated across tropical Asia
medical_use: Anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and digestive stimulant. Used across traditional Asian medicine systems for stomach complaints, nausea, and rheumatism. Galangin and other flavonoids have demonstrated antibacterial and antifungal activity.
historic_use: Reached medieval Europe via Arab traders and was a prized spice in 11th–14th century European cuisine, appearing in records of Charlemagne's royal gardens. Described by Hildegard von Bingen as the 'spice of life' given by God. In American Hoodoo it became 'chewing John' — chewed and spit near a courthouse to win legal cases.

name: Garlic
description: One of the most universally protective plants across all cultures — hung above doorways in Eastern Europe to ward off vampires and the evil eye; buried at crossroads to avert ill spirits.
nativity: Central Asia (likely Kyrgyzstan/Tajikistan region); cultivated for at least 7,000 years
medical_use: Antimicrobial, cardiovascular protective, and immunomodulatory. Allicin (formed when garlic is crushed) has broad-spectrum antimicrobial activity. Clinically studied for modest reduction in blood pressure and LDL cholesterol. Used extensively across traditional medicine systems worldwide.
historic_use: Workers building the Egyptian pyramids were paid in garlic — ancient records indicate a strike over garlic rations. Found in Tutankhamun's tomb. Dioscorides prescribed it for over 60 conditions. It was the primary protective herb of medieval Europe and is central to vampire folklore from Romania to East Asia.

name: Heather
description: White heather is the luckiest plant in Scottish tradition, said to grow only where fairies have trod. Carried for luck and to attract romance; burned in fairy contact workings on hillsides.
nativity: Scotland, Western Europe, Siberia; on acidic moorland and bogs
medical_use: Diuretic and mild antiseptic. Used in Scottish and Scandinavian folk medicine for urinary tract infections and kidney stones as a tea. Arbutin content gives mild antiseptic properties in the urinary tract. Also used for gout and rheumatism.
historic_use: The dominant plant of the Scottish and Irish highland moors. Used since Neolithic times to make heather ale — the 'Fraoch Ale' of Gaelic tradition. Heather thatch, bedding, brooms, and rope were staples of Scottish highland material culture. White heather has been a symbol of Highland luck since at least the time of Macbeth.

name: Holly
description: The sacred winter tree of the Celts — the Holly King rules the waning year. Hung at Yule for luck and protection, and placed near the bed for prophetic dreams of the new year.
safety: Holly berries are toxic — keep away from children and animals.
nativity: Western and southern Europe, northwestern Africa, southwestern Asia
medical_use: Leaves used as a febrifuge and diuretic in European folk medicine. South American species (Ilex paraguariensis — yerba mate) are used as stimulating beverages. Theobromine and caffeine are present in some Ilex species. Holly berries should not be used medicinally — they are emetic and purgative.
historic_use: In the Celtic calendar the Holly King rules the dark half of the year in eternal battle with the Oak King. Romans sent holly boughs as gifts during Saturnalia — the tradition was absorbed into Christmas decoration. The early Christian church adopted holly as a symbol of Christ's crown of thorns. One of the key plants of Druidic ceremony.

name: Honeysuckle
description: Crushed and rubbed on the forehead to heighten psychic ability. Twined around green candles to draw money; placed in sachets to keep out negative influences and preserve happy memories.
nativity: Europe, North Africa
medical_use: Antiviral, anti-inflammatory, and expectorant. European honeysuckle flowers used in folk medicine for coughs and fevers. Chinese honeysuckle (Lonicera japonica — jin yin hua) is extensively used in Traditional Chinese Medicine for heat-clearing and antiviral applications, including influenza.
historic_use: The twisting climbing habit of honeysuckle around saplings was seen as a symbol of love — it was used in love knots and bouquets. Medieval herbalists prescribed it for asthma and urinary complaints. In Celtic mythology it represents the hidden sweetness within difficulty. The scent intensifies at night to attract moths for pollination.

name: Horehound
description: An ancient herb of Hecate used to counter psychic attack and break enchantments. One of the oldest recorded magical and medicinal herbs — found dried in Egyptian tombs over 3,000 years old.
nativity: Mediterranean region, central Asia; widely naturalised worldwide
medical_use: Expectorant, antispasmodic, and bitter tonic. One of the oldest cough remedies in Western medicine. Marrubiin relaxes bronchial smooth muscle. Approved in German Commission E as a cough treatment. Still used in horehound candy and throat lozenges.
historic_use: The Ebers Papyrus (1550 BCE) lists it as a lung medicine. Used by the ancient Greeks and documented by Dioscorides, Pliny, and Galen. One of the bitter herbs of the Jewish Passover seder (maror) in some traditions. Used continuously in European pharmacy from antiquity through the 20th century.

name: Iris
description: Orris root — dried iris rhizome — is a classic fixative of love perfumery and a powerful tool for love divination. Named for the goddess of the rainbow who bridges worlds; used in sachets to attract love.
nativity: Mediterranean region; cultivated in Italy (Tuscany) for orris root production since the 15th century
medical_use: Orris root is primarily used as a perfume fixative rather than medicinally. Historically used as a mild diuretic and expectorant. The powdered root was used in dental powders. Iris versicolor (blue flag) root was used by Cherokee and Iroquois nations as an emetic and cathartic.
historic_use: The fleur-de-lis — stylized iris flower — has been the symbol of French royalty since the 6th-century Frankish kings. Named for Iris, messenger goddess of the rainbow and bridge between mortal and divine worlds. Cultivated at scale in the Chianti hills of Tuscany for orris root; the dried root smells of violets and is essential to classical perfumery.

name: Ivy
description: Sacred to Dionysus and associated with the inescapable creeping quality of Saturn. Carried by brides for fidelity; used in binding spells and to mark the sacred boundary of a working space.
safety: Berries are toxic. Sap may cause skin irritation.
nativity: Western and central Europe, northwestern Africa, southwestern Asia
medical_use: Expectorant and antispasmodic. Ivy leaf extract (Hedera helix) is an approved expectorant in European phytomedicine for bronchitis and productive coughs — clinically studied and widely sold in Germany. The saponins relax bronchial smooth muscle.
historic_use: Sacred to Dionysus — inn signs in ancient Greece and Rome featured ivy bushes to indicate wine was served (origin of 'the ivy bush'). The alternation of ivy and holly represented the eternal contest of the Holly and Oak Kings in Celtic tradition. Ivy was prohibited in early Christian churches as too Dionysian; later incorporated into Christmas greenery.

name: Jasmine
description: An intensely feminine lunar scent used across Asia in love and spiritual workings. Jasmine flowers placed on the altar attract spiritual love; added to dream pillows for prophetic visions.
nativity: Western China, Himalayas; Arabian jasmine (J. sambac) from South Asia
medical_use: Mild sedative and antidepressant in aromatherapy. Jasmine absolute used in high-end perfumery as a heart note. Studies show inhaling jasmine fragrance reduces anxiety and improves mood. Jasmine tea is made by scenting green tea with jasmine flowers — widely consumed in China for its calming properties.
historic_use: Used in Indian temple offerings and bridal garlands for thousands of years. Jasmine sambac is the national flower of the Philippines (sampaguita) and Indonesia (melati putih). The scent of jasmine was described in ancient Sanskrit texts as one of the divine fragrances. Central to the love poetry of Persia and the Mughal court.

name: Lotus
description: The supreme sacred flower of Egypt, India, and Buddhist tradition — rising pure from muddy water, it symbolizes the soul's awakening and divine protection. Used in workings of elevation and spiritual connection.
nativity: South Asia, Southeast Asia, Australia; cultivated throughout tropical and subtropical Asia
medical_use: Nutritive and anti-inflammatory. Seeds, roots, and leaves are all edible in Asian cuisines. Leaf extracts studied for antioxidant and antihyperglycemic properties. Lotus seeds used in Traditional Chinese Medicine as a heart tonic and for insomnia. Nuciferine from lotus leaves is studied as a mild antipsychotic.
historic_use: The lotus is perhaps the most sacred flower in the world. In ancient Egypt it represented the sun emerging from primordial waters — Ra rose from a lotus at the first dawn. In Hindu tradition the lotus is the seat of Brahma, Lakshmi, and Saraswati. The 'Om mani padme hum' mantra of Tibetan Buddhism translates as 'the jewel in the lotus.' The lotus has been in continuous sacred use for at least 5,000 years.

name: Meadowsweet
description: One of the three most sacred herbs of the Druids alongside vervain and water mint. Strewn on floors for joy; used in love divination at midsummer. Its scent lightens the heaviest heart.
nativity: Europe, western Asia
medical_use: Anti-inflammatory and analgesic. Contains salicylates — in 1897 Bayer's Felix Hoffmann derived acetylsalicylic acid (aspirin) partly from compounds found in meadowsweet (spiraeas). Used in folk medicine for headaches, fever, heartburn, and gastric ulcers.
historic_use: One of the three most sacred Druidic herbs alongside vervain and water mint. Strewn on floors at Beltane and used to scent mead — the old English name 'mead wort' reflects this. The very word 'aspirin' derives from Spiraea ulmaria (old name for meadowsweet) — the 'a' from acetyl, 'spir' from Spiraea. Found preserved in a Bronze Age burial alongside other plants in Scotland.

name: Mistletoe
description: The most sacred plant of the Druids — cut with a golden sickle, never allowed to touch the earth. Hung for Yule luck, carried against poisoning, and used in fertility rites. The plant that killed Baldur.
safety: Berries are toxic. Do not ingest.
nativity: Europe, northwestern Africa, western Asia; a hemiparasitic plant on various host trees
medical_use: Mistletoe extracts (Iscador, Helixor, Abnoba) are among the most studied complementary cancer treatments in Europe, especially in German-speaking countries. Used by anthroposophic physicians as adjuvant cancer therapy. Also studied for hypertension. Raw plant is toxic — only standardized extracts should be used medicinally.
historic_use: Pliny the Elder described the Druidic ritual of cutting mistletoe from oak with a golden sickle on the sixth day of the moon with great ceremony, catching it in a white cloak. It was called 'all-heal' — a universal cure. The Norse god Baldur was killed by a mistletoe dart. The Christmas kissing tradition comes from a Norse myth of reconciliation under mistletoe after Baldur's death.

name: Motherwort
description: The herb of the mother — used in workings of female empowerment, protection of children, and countering magic directed at the family. Carried to bring inner strength and calm the anxious heart.
safety: Avoid in pregnancy and with heart medications. Uterine stimulant.
nativity: Central Asia, southeastern Europe; naturalised widely in temperate regions
medical_use: Cardiotonic, uterine stimulant, and nervine. Used in European and Chinese herbal medicine for palpitations, anxiety, and menstrual regulation. Leonurine is studied for effects on cardiac muscle. Approved in Germany for nervous heart conditions and menstrual disorders.
historic_use: The genus name Leonurus (lion heart) and species name cardiaca (of the heart) reflect its ancient reputation as a heart herb. Used in medieval Europe for palpitations and uterine health. Traditionally given to women after childbirth. In 17th-century herbalist Nicholas Culpeper assigned it to Venus and praised it for 'making mothers joyful and settling their wombs.'

name: Mullein
description: The torchplant — its flower stalks were dipped in tallow and lit in ancient rites. Used in scrying and hedge-riding; the dried leaves are smoked in divination practices across multiple traditions.
nativity: Europe, northern Africa, western and central Asia
medical_use: Expectorant and demulcent. One of the most widely used respiratory herbs in Western folk medicine. Leaf tea and tinctures used for bronchitis, coughs, and asthma. Mullein oil in olive oil used topically for ear infections. Studies show anti-inflammatory and antiviral properties.
historic_use: Used since antiquity as a lamp wick — wicks were made from the woolly leaves and flower stalks dipped in tallow. Dioscorides prescribed it for lung diseases and toothache. Native Americans adopted it for respiratory conditions and smoked the leaves ceremonially after contact with European settlers introduced the plant. The 'graveyard dust' of Hoodoo.

name: Myrtle
description: Sacred to Aphrodite — worn by brides since antiquity and used in workings of love and marriage. Myrtle carried in the wedding bouquet ensures fidelity; grown near the home it brings lasting peace.
nativity: Mediterranean region, Middle East, North Africa
medical_use: Antiseptic, antispasmodic, and expectorant. Myrtle essential oil used in European phytomedicine for respiratory conditions and sinusitis. Myrtle leaves used in traditional medicine of North Africa and Middle East for urinary tract infections, diabetes management, and as an astringent.
historic_use: Sacred to Aphrodite and Venus — according to myth she hid behind a myrtle bush emerging from the sea. Central to Greek and Roman wedding ceremonies; Roman brides wore myrtle wreaths. The wedding bouquet tradition of British royalty includes a sprig from the myrtle planted at Osborne House by Queen Victoria's mother. Used continuously in Jewish Sukkot ceremonies (one of the four species).

name: Nutmeg
description: Whole nutmegs carried in the pocket bring luck in gambling; half a nutmeg worn as a pendant attracts a faithful lover. Used in travel sachets and added to prosperity workings to multiply results.
safety: Toxic in large amounts — use culinary quantities for magical work, never medicinal doses.
nativity: Banda Islands (Maluku/Spice Islands), Indonesia
medical_use: Carminative, digestive stimulant, and mild analgesic. Used in Ayurveda for digestive disorders, insomnia (in small doses), and diarrhea. Myristicin is the principal compound — hallucinogenic and toxic in large doses. Nutmeg oil used topically for rheumatic pain.
historic_use: The Banda Islands were the world's sole source of nutmeg until the 17th century — which made them one of the most fiercely contested pieces of land in history. The Dutch VOC committed the Banda massacre (1621) to seize the monopoly, killing most of the 15,000-strong population. Nutmeg was so valuable in medieval Europe that it was used to pay rent and given as costly gifts.

name: Oak
description: The king of the forest and the sacred tree of the Druids, Jupiter, Thor, and Zeus alike. Acorns are carried for long life and fertility; oak bark burned for strength and endurance in any working.
nativity: Europe, Asia Minor; one of the most ecologically dominant trees of the Northern Hemisphere
medical_use: Astringent and anti-inflammatory. Oak bark is an approved European phytomedicine for inflammatory skin conditions, hemorrhoids, and mild diarrhea. Tannins are the primary active compounds. Oak galls (caused by wasps) were historically used as powerful astringents in wound care.
historic_use: Druid literally means 'oak-knower' (from Celtic *dru-wid — oak wisdom). Sacred to Zeus (Dodona), Jupiter, Thor, and the Dagda. The oak and the mistletoe together formed the center of Druidic ceremony. English warships, the Royal Navy's maritime dominance, and the nation's forests were inseparably linked — 'Heart of Oak' became Britain's naval anthem. Acorns were a staple food across Eurasia for millennia before grain agriculture.

name: Orange Peel
description: Dried orange peel added to love sachets and prosperity workings, or burned to bring solar energy and joy into the home. Combined with cinnamon for powerful money-drawing magic.
nativity: Southern China, northeast India; hybrid of pomelo and mandarin
medical_use: Rich in vitamin C and bioflavonoids. Orange peel contains d-limonene, studied for antifungal, antibacterial, and potential anticancer properties. Dried peel (chen pi) is used in Traditional Chinese Medicine for digestive complaints and phlegm conditions. Neroli oil (from the blossom) is used in aromatherapy for anxiety.
historic_use: Sweet oranges reached the Arab world via trade with China around the 10th century CE and Europe via Arab traders and the Crusades. The word 'orange' derives from Sanskrit nāranga through Persian and Arabic. Oranges were luxury gifts — giving oranges studded with cloves (pomanders) was a Christmas gift tradition in Elizabethan England. The orange groves of Andalusia were planted by the Moors.

name: Peony
description: A peony root kept in the home wards against evil spirits and the evil eye. Chinese tradition considers the peony the king of flowers, associated with high honor, nobility, and good fortune.
nativity: Southern Europe (P. officinalis); China (P. lactiflora)
medical_use: White peony root (bai shao) is one of the most important herbs in Traditional Chinese Medicine — used for liver conditions, menstrual irregularity, and muscle spasms. Paeoniflorin is anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic. Extensively studied in Japan and China for autoimmune and inflammatory conditions.
historic_use: Named for Paeon, physician to the Olympian gods, who healed Ares and Hades with it. Theophrastus described elaborate harvest rituals requiring a woodpecker. Planted in medieval monastery gardens as a protective herb against demons and nightmares. The peony has been the symbol of prosperity in Chinese court culture for over 1,500 years; it is now the unofficial national flower of China.

name: Black Pepper
description: A fierce protective spice — sprinkled in doorways to ward off unwanted visitors and thrown at the heels of someone who has wronged you to send them away permanently.
nativity: Kerala, southwestern India (Malabar Coast)
medical_use: Carminative, stimulant, and bioavailability enhancer. Piperine significantly increases absorption of other nutrients and drugs (including curcumin and certain medications). Used in Ayurveda as a primary digestive herb. Demonstrates antimicrobial and antioxidant properties.
historic_use: Called 'black gold' — the single most traded spice in history and a key driver of the Age of Exploration. Attila the Hun demanded 3,000 pounds of pepper as part of Rome's ransom in 408 CE. Pepper was used as currency in medieval Europe. The Sanskrit epithet yavanapriya ('beloved of the Greeks') reflects how eagerly Greek and Roman traders sought it. The search for a direct sea route to the pepper coast of India led Columbus to the Americas.

name: Pine
description: Pine resin and needles burned to purify and protect. Associated with longevity in Asian traditions and resilience in Celtic lore. Pine cones are ancient fertility charms and symbols of regeneration.
nativity: Northern Eurasia, from Scotland to Siberia; the most widespread pine species in the world
medical_use: Expectorant, antimicrobial, and anti-inflammatory. Pine needle tea is high in vitamin C — used by Indigenous peoples and early European explorers to prevent scurvy. Pine resin (turpentine) applied topically as a rubefacient for rheumatism. Pine bark extract (Pycnogenol) studied for antioxidant and cardiovascular effects.
historic_use: The pine was sacred to Attis and Cybele — pine trees were carried in procession and decorated in Roman rites of spring. Japan's most sacred Shinto shrine (Ise) is marked by ancient hinoki cypress and pine. In Celtic tradition the Scots pine is one of the chieftain trees of the Ogham forest. The resinous pine torch (pinea) lit the way through the dark in antiquity; pine shaped early civilization more than almost any other tree.

name: Pomegranate
description: The fruit of Persephone — eating six seeds bound her to the underworld for half the year. Used in workings of death and rebirth, ancestor communication, and fertility. Count the seeds for divination.
nativity: Iran, Afghanistan, northern India; cultivated throughout the Mediterranean for at least 5,000 years
medical_use: Antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and cardioprotective. Pomegranate juice is among the most studied fruits for cardiovascular health — studies show reduction in blood pressure and LDL oxidation. Punicalagins are potent polyphenols. Pomegranate peel extract has demonstrated broad-spectrum antimicrobial activity.
historic_use: One of the seven species of the biblical Land of Israel. Pomegranates decorated the pillars of Solomon's Temple and the hem of the high priest's robe. The name 'Punic apple' reflects Roman association with Carthage (Punica). At the heart of the Eleusinian Mysteries — the myth of Persephone eating pomegranate seeds in the underworld explained the seasons. Depicted in Egyptian tomb art from at least 1500 BCE.

name: Poppy
description: Sacred to Hypnos and Demeter — a flower of forgetting and of the harvest. Poppy seeds are added to sleep sachets and scattered to confuse those who wish you harm. Red poppies honor the war dead; white poppies honor all ancestors.
safety: Opium poppy (Papaver somniferum) is legal to grow ornamentally in most US states. Extracting opium is federally illegal.
nativity: Eastern Mediterranean, Middle East (P. somniferum); temperate Europe and Asia (P. rhoeas)
medical_use: Opium poppy latex contains morphine and codeine — the basis of modern opioid pharmaceuticals. Morphine remains the gold standard for severe pain relief in modern medicine. Poppy seed oil is used in cooking. The corn poppy has minimal medicinal use.
historic_use: Evidence of opium poppy cultivation dates to Neolithic Lake Dwellers (Switzerland, c. 3400 BCE). The Sumerians called it hul gil — 'plant of joy.' Egyptians and Greeks used it for pain relief and ritual. The entire 19th-century Opium Wars between Britain and China were fought over the East India Company's opium trade. The red poppy became the symbol of WWI remembrance after John McCrae's poem 'In Flanders Fields' (1915).

name: Red Clover
description: Four-leaf clovers are the supreme luck charm of European folk tradition. Three-leaf clovers were sacred to the Celts as symbols of the threefold goddess. Used in love sachets and carried to ensure fidelity.
nativity: Europe, western Asia, northwest Africa
medical_use: Isoflavone-rich herb studied for menopausal symptom relief, particularly hot flashes. Phytoestrogenic compounds (formononetin, biochanin A) provide mild hormonal modulation. Red clover extracts (Promensil) are clinically evaluated for bone density support in postmenopausal women.
historic_use: Clover was the emblem of the Celtic Brehon law protecting triads. The trefoil was adopted by St. Patrick to explain the Christian Trinity to the Irish. In European folk tradition, the four-leaf clover offers protection against witchcraft — the Victorians made a national pastime of searching for them in fields. Red clover is one of the most important agricultural forage plants, responsible for fixing nitrogen in pastures.

name: Rowan
description: The strongest protective tree in Celtic and Norse tradition — rowan crosses bound with red thread ward witchcraft and ill spirits. Rowan berries are sacred to Brighid and used in workings of foresight.
safety: Raw berries are mildly toxic. Cook before consuming.
nativity: Europe, western Asia; tolerates colder climates than most trees
medical_use: Rowan berries are high in vitamin C and mild sorbic acid (named for the tree). Cooked berries used in jams and wines in Scandinavian and British folk medicine for vitamin C supplementation. Bark was used as a mild astringent and febrifuge. Raw berries cause gastrointestinal distress.
historic_use: In Scottish and Irish folk magic, a cross of rowan bound with red thread is the most potent charm against witchcraft and the evil eye — placed above doorways on May Day. Sacred to Thor — lightning protection. In Norse mythology Yggdrasil is variously described as ash or rowan. The Druids burned it in ritual fires for divination. In Welsh tradition (Cad Goddeu — Battle of the Trees), the rowan is listed among the warrior trees.

name: Saffron
description: The most valuable spice in the world — sacred to Hecate, Apollo, and the divine feminine across cultures. Burned to raise the wind, used in love potions, and worn to strengthen psychic sight.
safety: Very large quantities are toxic. Use culinary amounts in magical work.
nativity: Likely originated in Greece or Southwest Asia; now primarily cultivated in Iran, Spain, and Kashmir
medical_use: Antidepressant, antioxidant, and antiemetic. Multiple clinical trials demonstrate efficacy for mild to moderate depression comparable to SSRIs. Studied for PMS, Alzheimer's disease, and age-related macular degeneration. Crocin and safranal are the primary active compounds.
historic_use: Saffron has been cultivated for over 3,500 years — Minoan frescoes at Akrotiri (c. 1600 BCE) show women harvesting it for a goddess.
