Used to dye the robes of Buddhist monks and the bridal veils of ancient Greece. The most expensive spice by weight — it takes 150,000 flowers (450 hours of hand labor) to produce one kilogram. Alexander the Great bathed in it and credited it with healing his wounds.

name: Solomon's Seal
description: The root used to seal and bind magical workings — carried for protection or used to consecrate ritual tools. King Solomon's seal and signature are said to be written in the root's markings.
safety: Berries are mildly toxic. Do not confuse with false Solomon's Seal when wildcrafting.
nativity: Europe, temperate Asia
medical_use: Demulcent and anti-inflammatory. Root preparations used in traditional European and Chinese medicine for tendon and joint injuries, bruises, and respiratory dryness. Polygonatum odoratum (jade bamboo / yu zhu) is a major Chinese herb for Yin deficiency conditions including dry cough and thirst.
historic_use: The circular scars left where each year's stem falls from the rhizome were said to resemble the Seal of Solomon. Dioscorides and Gerard both described it as a remedy for broken bones and bruises. Long used in Chinese medicine as a longevity and Yin-nourishing tonic. The 'seal' markings gave rise to the belief that the root could seal and bind workings of magic.

name: Star Anise
description: The eight-pointed pods are burned before divination to sharpen psychic sight. Hung in the four directions to purify a space; carried as luck charms in Asian and Hoodoo traditions alike.
nativity: Southwestern China, northeastern Vietnam
medical_use: Carminative, antimicrobial, and expectorant. Shikimic acid extracted from star anise is the primary precursor used to synthesize oseltamivir (Tamiflu), the leading antiviral medication for influenza. Anethole gives antimicrobial properties. Used in Chinese medicine for abdominal cold pain and digestive complaints.
historic_use: Used in Chinese cooking and medicine for over 3,000 years. Reached Europe via the Silk Road in the late 16th century. Became a key flavoring of European anise-flavored liqueurs (Pernod, pastis, ouzo, sambuca). The global importance of star anise increased dramatically when it became the industrial source of shikimic acid for Tamiflu production in the early 2000s.

name: Tobacco
description: The most sacred plant to many Indigenous peoples of the Americas — offered at altars, carried as prayer bundles, and used to open communication with ancestors and spirits. In many traditions, offering tobacco is the proper way to ask something of the earth, an elder, or a spirit.
safety: Deeply sacred to Indigenous traditions — approach with respect. Not for casual use.
nativity: South America; N. tabacum from Bolivia/Argentina region, N. rustica from Andes/Mexico
medical_use: Not medicinal in the conventional sense. Nicotine has been studied for cognitive enhancement and Parkinson's disease. N. rustica (mapacho) is used in Amazonian shamanic practice and is a component of rapé (ritual snuff). Commercial tobacco's well-established health risks are severe and extensively documented.
historic_use: Archaeological evidence dates tobacco use in the Americas to at least 3,500 years ago. The sacred pipe (chanupa) of Plains peoples is among the most holy objects in Indigenous North America. Columbus encountered tobacco being smoked in Cuba in 1492. Within a century it had spread globally. N. rustica (the wild-crafted ceremonial tobacco of Amazonia and Mesoamerica) is far stronger than commercial tobacco and used in shamanic healing traditions.

name: Turmeric
description: A sacred golden spice used in Hindu ritual — smeared on the body before ceremony and offered to the deities. In folk magic it is used to attract wealth and strengthen all solar workings.
nativity: South Asia (likely southern India or Southeast Asia)
medical_use: Anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and hepatoprotective. Curcumin is the primary active compound — extensively studied for inflammatory conditions, metabolic syndrome, and neuroprotection. Bioavailability is poor without piperine (black pepper). Used in Ayurveda for joint conditions, liver disease, skin disorders, and wound healing.
historic_use: Used in Hindu ritual for at least 4,000 years — turmeric paste is applied to the bride and groom before weddings (haldi ceremony) to bless, purify, and beautify. Among the sacred herbs of the Vedas. The yellow-gold color associates it with solar deities. Marco Polo compared it to saffron. It has been used continuously in South Asian cooking and medicine for longer than most spices have been recorded.

name: Vetiver
description: A deeply earthy grass root used in Hoodoo and New Orleans tradition for luck in money and love. Added to mojo bags and floor washes to draw prosperity and protect against theft and misfortune.
nativity: South Asia (India); widely cultivated in tropical regions worldwide
medical_use: Sedative and anxiolytic in aromatherapy. Vetiver essential oil is used in Ayurveda for cooling inflammatory conditions (it is a 'cooling' herb in Ayurvedic terms), anxiety, insomnia, and skin conditions. Used as a root mat cooler — vetiver screens soaked in water cool air as a pre-electricity air conditioner across South Asia.
historic_use: Called khus in Hindi — woven into screens and fans for centuries in India as a cooling fragrance in summer heat. One of the most valued perfumery base notes in Western perfumery — its deep, smoky, earthy scent fixes other fragrances and has been used in men's colognes since the 19th century. In Haiti and the American South, vetiver root entered Hoodoo as a luck and protection herb.

name: Violet
description: Combined with lavender in a potent love sachet. Violets are fairy flowers — planted in the garden to invite fairy presence and tucked into charm bags to grant wishes and bring gentle healing.
nativity: Europe, Asia; widely cultivated worldwide
medical_use: Expectorant, demulcent, and mild anti-inflammatory. Violet leaf tea and syrup used in traditional European medicine for coughs, bronchitis, and sore throats. Violet contains mucilages, salicylates, and rutin. Violet flowers are also edible and high in vitamin C.
historic_use: Athens adopted the violet as its emblem — festivals were held when violets bloomed. Napoleon chose it as his emblem after exile: supporters would ask 'do you like violets?' as a secret recognition phrase; his return was announced as 'la violette a reparu.' Napoleon famously requested violets be planted on Josephine's grave and reportedly died with violets from her garden in a locket. Sacred to Aphrodite, Persephone, and Io (Zeus transformed her into a cow and created violets for her to eat).

name: White Willow
description: A tree of the Moon and deep water — associated with Hecate, Cerridwen, and all moon goddesses. Willow wands serve lunar workings; the bark is placed in sachets for healing rites and grief work.
nativity: Central and southern Europe, central Asia
medical_use: Analgesic and anti-inflammatory. Salicin in willow bark is converted to salicylic acid in the body — this provided the template for aspirin (acetylsalicylic acid). Clinically studied for lower back pain and osteoarthritis. Gentler on the stomach than aspirin due to slower conversion rate.
historic_use: Hippocrates wrote of using willow bark and leaves for pain and fever. Used for 2,400 years across Greek, Roman, and Islamic medicine. In 1763, Rev. Edward Stone formally reported its efficacy to the Royal Society, which led directly to the isolation of salicin in 1828 and the synthesis of aspirin in 1899. Sacred to Hecate, Persephone, and Cerridwen across Greek, Roman, and Celtic traditions.

name: Witch Hazel
description: The classic dowsing rod of American folk magic — forked witch hazel branches were used to find water and buried treasure. Burned to reveal hidden things and protect against spirits that steal energy.
nativity: Eastern North America
medical_use: Astringent and anti-inflammatory. Witch hazel distillate is one of the most widely sold OTC skincare ingredients in the United States — used for hemorrhoids, bruises, minor skin irritation, and as an aftershave. Hamamelidotannin and gallic acid are primary active compounds. Applied topically for varicose veins in European phytomedicine.
historic_use: Used by numerous Indigenous North American peoples — including the Lenape, Cherokee, and Iroquois — for skin inflammation, sore muscles, and eye complaints. Introduced to European settlers who adopted it widely. Witch hazel water (produced by steam distillation) became a household staple by the 19th century. The 'witch' in its name may come from Middle English wiche — flexible, bendable — reflecting its use as a dowsing rod.

name: Wood Betony
description: An ancient protective herb — the Romans counted its virtues against 47 diseases. Planted in churchyards to protect the dead; scattered around the home to create a barrier against negative spirits.
nativity: Europe, western Asia; found in dry woodland and hedgerows
medical_use: Nervine, mild astringent, and digestive bitter. Used in European herbal medicine for headaches, anxiety, and nervous exhaustion. Betaine and stachydrine are active compounds. Used as a bitter digestive tonic and mild anxiolytic in modern Western herbal practice.
historic_use: The Roman physician Antonius Musa (physician to Augustus Caesar) wrote an entire treatise on betony listing 47 conditions it could treat. A Roman proverb advised 'sell your coat and buy betony.' Planted in churchyards across medieval England to protect the dead and ward off evil spirits. Geoffrey of Monmouth listed it among the herbs of Merlin.

name: Ylang Ylang
description: The tropical flower of seduction — in Philippine tradition scattered on the beds of newlyweds. Used in love oil blends and beauty magic; the intensely sweet scent calms anxiety and invites sensual connection.
nativity: Philippines, Indonesia, Malaysia; tropical Asia
medical_use: Anxiolytic and mild antihypertensive. Ylang ylang essential oil inhalation demonstrated reduction in blood pressure and heart rate in clinical studies. Used in aromatherapy for anxiety, depression, and sexual dysfunction. Applied topically in hair oils for scalp conditions.
historic_use: The name ylang ylang derives from Filipino ilang meaning 'wilderness' — the flower of the wilderness. Scattered on the marriage bed in Philippine and Indonesian tradition. Macassar oil — a 19th-century European men's hair pomade — was made from ylang ylang-infused coconut oil from the Macassar region of Sulawesi. The flowers are used to make flower garlands for greeting honored guests across Southeast Asia.

name: Coltsfoot
description: One of the earliest herbs to bloom in spring — burned in divination workings to open psychic channels and used in love sachets. A traditional lung herb of healers; the hoof-shaped leaf gives the plant its name.
safety: Contains pyrrolizidine alkaloids — toxic to the liver in large doses. For spell jars and sachets only. Do not ingest or smoke regularly.
nativity: Europe, Asia; naturalised in North America
medical_use: Expectorant and antispasmodic. One of the oldest recorded cough remedies in European medicine. Mucilages soothe irritated mucous membranes. Now largely restricted due to pyrrolizidine alkaloid content — tussilagine and isotussilagine are hepatotoxic with chronic use. PA-free preparations available in Germany.
historic_use: The genus name Tussilago means 'cough dispeller' in Latin. French apothecary signs traditionally depicted a coltsfoot leaf as the symbol of their trade. Used continuously from ancient Greece through the 19th century as the primary herb for coughs and bronchitis. One of the first flowers of spring — its yellow flowers appear before the leaves, earning it the name 'son before the father' in some European traditions.

name: Blue Lotus
description: The sacred flower of ancient Egypt — depicted in countless tomb paintings as an offering to the gods. Associated with Ra, Osiris, and Nefertem the god of the blue lotus. Consumed in ritual wine for divine communion and to induce visionary states.
safety: Safe for spell jars, sachets, and altar work. Legal in the US though used as a mild psychoactive entheogen. Do not combine with depressants, SSRIs, or MAOIs.
nativity: Egypt, East Africa; widely cultivated in India and Southeast Asia
medical_use: Mild psychoactive — nuciferine and aporphine alkaloids act on dopamine and serotonin receptors. Used in traditional Egyptian and Ayurvedic medicine as a mild sedative, aphrodisiac, and anxiolytic. Studied for anti-inflammatory and antihyperglycemic properties. Classed as a novel psychoactive substance in some jurisdictions.
historic_use: The Blue Lotus is perhaps the most depicted plant in ancient Egyptian art — shown in tomb paintings at Luxor, Karnak, and Amarna from at least 1500 BCE. Nefertem, god of the lotus, emerged from the primordial waters holding a lotus. Scenes on papyri and tomb walls show figures holding lotus flowers to their noses — interpreted as ritual inhalation for visionary purposes. Mixed into wine for Dionysian-style ecstatic ritual.

name: Kava Kava
description: The ceremonial drink of the Pacific Islands — kava ceremonies mark every important occasion in Fiji, Tonga, and Vanuatu. The root is pounded to extract a mildly psychoactive drink that induces calm, social connection, and at high doses mild visionary states.
safety: Safe for spell jars and altar work. Legal in the US. May cause liver damage with heavy or chronic use. Avoid with alcohol and liver medications. Approach the sacred use of kava with respect for the cultures it comes from.
nativity: Western Pacific Islands; cultivated throughout Polynesia, Melanesia, and Micronesia
medical_use: Anxiolytic and mild sedative. Kavalactones are the active compounds. Multiple clinical trials demonstrate efficacy for generalized anxiety disorder comparable to benzodiazepines without tolerance or dependence risk. Rare hepatotoxicity cases reported with concentrated extracts — traditional water-based preparations appear safer. Approved for anxiety in German Commission E.
historic_use: Kava has been cultivated and used ceremonially in the Pacific for over 3,000 years. In Fiji (yaqona), Tonga, Samoa, and Vanuatu, kava ceremonies are the foundation of diplomacy, community gathering, and spiritual practice — no significant event occurs without it. Captain Cook documented kava ceremonies in 1768. The German Commission E approved kava extracts for anxiety in 1990; this was suspended in 2002 over hepatotoxicity concerns and later partially reversed.

name: Pennyroyal
description: A fierce little mint with a long history in female magic — used to protect against hostile spirits, clear the mind, and mark rites of passage. Hung in the home to drive away fleas and spirits alike.
safety: Safe for closed spell jars and sachets. A powerful uterine stimulant — do not ingest; causes miscarriage and liver failure in even moderate doses. Pennyroyal essential oil has killed adults. Keep sealed and away from pregnant practitioners.
nativity: Europe, North Africa, Middle East
medical_use: Historically used as an emmenagogue and abortifacient — the primary reason for its 'restricted' status. Pulegone is the toxic compound responsible for both the uterine-stimulating and hepatotoxic effects. No current safe medicinal use. The essential oil is lethal in doses as small as 10 ml.
historic_use: Used as an emmenagogue (to stimulate menstruation) and abortifacient since ancient Greek and Roman times — Dioscorides, Pliny, and Soranus all describe this use. Steeped in controversy as one of the traditional herbs of 'female control.' Used as a flea repellent across medieval Europe (floors were strewn with it). The 1994 death of a Florida woman from pennyroyal essential oil led to renewed warnings.

name: Tansy
description: An ancient herb of immortality — rubbed on the body of the dead in medieval tradition to preserve them and ward off insects. Used in burial rites, immortality workings, and as a bitter Passover herb in some traditions.
safety: Safe for closed spell jars and sealed altar work. Do not ingest — tansy oil is highly toxic, causing convulsions and liver damage. Contains thujone. Strong uterine stimulant; keep away from pregnant practitioners.
nativity: Europe and temperate Asia; naturalised in North America
medical_use: No current safe medicinal use. Historically used as a vermifuge (worm expellant), emmenagogue, and abortifacient. Thujone and camphor are the primary toxic compounds. Used in very diluted preparations as a topical insect repellent historically.
historic_use: In medieval Christian tradition, tansy cakes (tansy puddings) were eaten at Easter as a symbolic bitter herb recalling the Passover, and in some Sephardic Jewish communities tansy served as a Passover bitter herb. Bodies were packed in tansy to slow decomposition and repel insects — the Greek mythological figure Ganymede was made immortal by the gods using tansy. Cultivated in every medieval monastery garden across Europe.

name: Bloodroot
description: A North American woodland plant whose root bleeds vivid red-orange sap — used in Hoodoo tradition as a powerful love-drawing and uncrossing herb. The dried root is carried in a red flannel mojo bag or placed under the doorstep to bring love and ward hexes.
safety: Safe for spell jars, sealed sachets, and mojo bags. Do not ingest — toxic, causing vomiting and at high doses serious systemic harm. Avoid prolonged skin contact with fresh root sap.
nativity: Eastern North America (Nova Scotia to Florida, west to the Great Plains)
medical_use: Sanguinarine from bloodroot is used in dental products as an antimicrobial plaque inhibitor (Viadent mouthwash). Historically used by Indigenous peoples for skin conditions, warts, and as an escharotic (tissue-destroying) agent. The escharotic use is particularly dangerous — marketed as 'black salve' for skin cancer, it causes severe tissue destruction and is not a safe or approved cancer treatment.
historic_use: Used by Algonquin, Cherokee, Iroquois, and many other Indigenous nations as a dye, face paint for ceremony and warfare, and as a medicine for respiratory conditions. The vivid red sap was used to dye baskets and textiles. In Hoodoo tradition it entered as a love-drawing root, planted near the home or carried in a mojo bag with partner's name to draw them close.

name: White Bryony
description: The English mandrake — its forked root was used as a mandrake substitute in British folk magic and shaped into poppets. Associated with prosperity workings, weather magic, and binding spells.
safety: Safe for sealed poppet work and spell jars. Do not ingest — extremely toxic; as few as 40 berries have caused adult deaths. Wear gloves when handling the root. Do not burn.
nativity: Europe, North Africa, southwestern Asia
medical_use: No current medicinal use — too toxic. Cucurbitacins are the primary toxic compounds, causing severe gastrointestinal inflammation. Used homeopathically as Bryonia for joint pain, pleurisy, and constipation (at extreme dilutions only). The root was used as a drastic cathartic and emetic in medieval medicine.
historic_use: Because true mandrake (Mandragora) did not grow in Britain, white bryony root was fashioned to resemble the human form and sold as 'English mandrake' by cunning folk and herb sellers from the medieval period onward. Gerard's Herbal (1597) explicitly condemns the deception. Associated with weather working and prosperity magic in British folk tradition.

name: Iboga
description: The sacrament of the Bwiti people of Gabon and Cameroon — consumed in massive initiatory doses over multi-day ceremonies to contact ancestors and undergo death-and-rebirth transformation. One of the most psychologically powerful visionary plants known, a true initiation sacrament in the deepest sense.
safety: Schedule I in the US and controlled in many countries. Legal in some countries for religious use. Iboga ceremonies carry real physical risk including cardiac events. This entry is for spiritual and cultural reference only.
nativity: Equatorial West Africa (Gabon, Cameroon, Republic of Congo)
medical_use: Ibogaine (the primary alkaloid) is under serious clinical investigation for treatment of opioid addiction and alcoholism. Studies show single-dose ibogaine can interrupt opioid withdrawal and reduce cravings for months. Cardiac risks (QT prolongation) require medical screening. Legal for addiction treatment in Canada, Mexico, New Zealand, and several other countries. Schedule I in the US.
historic_use: The Bwiti religion of Gabon and Cameroon uses iboga in a multi-day initiation ceremony (Bwiti initiation) that is considered a death-and-rebirth experience — one undergoes a visionary journey to the land of the ancestors. This ceremony is both the most important event in a person's spiritual life and a community-binding rite. Knowledge of iboga was held for centuries within the Bwiti before its active compound, ibogaine, was isolated in 1901 and its addiction-interruption effects were discovered in the 1960s.
